title: wait: look for exit code in stopped state
body:
Make sure to look for the container's exit code when it's in stopped state.  With `--restart=always`, the container seems to stay in the stopped state which led the wait logic to loop until the 20 seconds timeout for the cleanup process to have finished kicks in.

Also defensively make sure to loop when the container is in stopped state but no exit code has been written yet.

Add a regression test to make sure Podman doesn't wait more than 20 seconds.  Even on a CI machine under high load I expect it to take much much much less than that, so I do not expect this test to flake in the future.

Test failures seem consistent
Yep, looking into it atm.
Not yet there. Will take a look later (and probably tomorrow).
I begin to think this is not the right approach.  It's probably better to think of letting a `--restart=always` container transition to exited.

OK, new attempt which makes sense - at least in my head.  Now, we're only looking up the exit code if the container is stopped and has the always restart policy.  Only in this case, the container won't transition to exited.

This seemed easier than trying to make such a container transition to exited.

@edsantiago @Luap99 PTanotherL
It's looking better now.

Tentatively LGTM, but I genuinely have no idea what `podman wait` even means in the context of a spinning container.  I would be OK with having `podman wait` time out with an error. I'm also OK with this. 

Whatever the approach, I think this is worth documenting in the man page.

> Tentatively LGTM, but I genuinely have no idea what podman wait even means in the context of a spinning container. I would be OK with having podman wait time out with an error. I'm also OK with this.

I think Podman should behave as Docker does, which it now does.

> Whatever the approach, I think this is worth documenting in the man page.

I am open to suggestions but do not know what to add to the current man page.

```diff
diff --git a/docs/source/markdown/podman-wait.1.md.in b/docs/source/markdown/podman-wait.1.md.in
index db3ce0d54..ee9d7e8c0 100644
--- a/docs/source/markdown/podman-wait.1.md.in
+++ b/docs/source/markdown/podman-wait.1.md.in
@@ -55,6 +55,19 @@ $ podman wait --ignore does-not-exist
 -1
 \```
 
+As a special case, `wait` on a continuously-restarting container will
+return immediately with the most recent return code:
+```
+$ podman run -d --name test --restart always alpine sh -c 'exit $RANDOM'
+$ for i in 1 2 3;do time podman wait test;done
+178
+podman wait test  0.03s user 0.01s system 61% cpu 0.061 total
+137
+podman wait test  0.02s user 0.02s system 55% cpu 0.069 total
+155
+podman wait test  0.03s user 0.01s system 64% cpu 0.064 total
+```
+
 ## SEE ALSO
 **[podman(1)](podman.1.md)**
 
```

> +As a special case, `wait` on a continuously-restarting container will
+return immediately with the most recent return code:

I don't think that "immediately" is correct.  Ultimately, it's a race that can - if badly timed - lead to `wait` to never return. Somehow similar if another process would run `container start` at a high frequency.

@Luap99 WDYT?

> > +As a special case, `wait` on a continuously-restarting container will
> > +return immediately with the most recent return code:
> 
> I don't think that "immediately" is correct. Ultimately, it's a race that can - if badly timed - lead to `wait` to never return. Somehow similar if another process would run `container start` at a high frequency.
> 
> @Luap99 WDYT?

correct it is always racy, it may or may not exit on the first restart we have no way to guarantee this.

Okay, how about this?
```
NOTE: this is a poll command; it does not actually detect state
transitions nor does it guarantee that a container is stopped.
Running `podman wait` on a on a continuously-restarting container,
such as one created by `podman kube play`, is meaningless and
unpredictable. It is guaranteed to complete within twenty seconds,
and to output some exit code from the container, but that exit code
may not bear much relation to the real world.
\```
$ podman run -d --name test --restart on-failure alpine sh -c 'exit $RANDOM'
$ for i in 1 2 3;do time  podman wait test;done
243
podman wait test  0.07s user 0.06s system 0% cpu 20.276 total
119
podman wait test  0.07s user 0.06s system 0% cpu 20.279 total
0
podman wait test  0.04s user 0.04s system 0% cpu 9.398 total
```

I'm fine with Ed's suggestions,  but I would like some tweaks to the verbiage:

"on a on a continuously-restarting" to "on a continuously-restarting" 

" bear much relation to the real world" to "bear much relation to the actual state of the monitored container in this circumstance."
Happy green test buttons fwiw

Some clarifications on the proposed paragraph.  Just to make sure we're all on the same page.

> NOTE: this is a poll command; it does not actually detect state
transitions nor does it guarantee that a container is stopped.

It does guarantee that.  But it does not forbid the container to be restarted.  The container is even locked when checking the state.

> Running `podman wait` on a on a continuously-restarting container,
such as one created by `podman kube play`, is meaningless and
unpredictable. 

Isn't that up to the user to decide whether it's meaningless?  I think we need to highlight the `--restart=only` behavior (default in `kube play`) and leave interpreting the usefulness up to users.

> It is guaranteed to complete within twenty seconds,
and to output some exit code from the container, but that exit code
may not bear much relation to the real world.

It does not guarantee to complete within twenty seconds.  The timeout is only used when the container's conmon process is dead but its exit code isn't written within 20 sec; ultimately to protect against infinite loops in case conmon crashes etc.
Added a note to the man page, PTAL at https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/18581/files#diff-128f91965e6d9e997a6522cb6fa8023bf10bfea259c9d5494fa2494800ba41bd
@edsantiago PTanotherL
LGTM! Thank you! @containers/podman-maintainers PTanotherL
/lgtm
